1: Solomon began building 'on Mount Moriah, where YHWH had appeared to David his father, at the place that David had appointed, on the threshing floor of Ornan the Jebusite.' The mountain specification is the Chronicler's unique addition: Moriah connects the temple site to Gen 22 (Abraham's near-sacrifice of Isaac on 'one of the mountains' in the land of Moriah). The temple is built on the mountain of substitution — where the ram replaced Isaac — and tradition from the early church period locates Golgotha in the same Jerusalem hill complex. Sacrifice, substitution, divine provision, and resurrection are all concentrated at the same sacred geography.
2: The construction begins in the second month of Solomon's fourth year. The precision of the date anchors the typological narrative in history. The temple-building required preparation, materials, and a chosen moment — Christ's incarnation similarly came 'when the fullness of time had come' (Galatians 4:4), the divinely appointed moment after centuries of preparation.
3: The foundations: sixty cubits long, twenty cubits wide — exactly double the tabernacle's dimensions (30 × 10 cubits). The doubling signals escalation — not replacement but fulfillment. 'Something greater than the temple is here' (Matthew 12:6): Christ is not merely the temple-in-double but its complete transcendence.
4: The vestibule (ʾûlām) — the transition space between outside and inside, court and sanctuary. Christ is the transition point: 'I am the door. If anyone enters by me, he will be saved' (John 10:9); all access to the Father passes through the one who stood as the gateway between profane and sacred.
5: The main hall paneled with cypress and overlaid with gold — gold adorning the organic wood. The combination of organic material (wood) and precious covering (gold) suggests the union of humanity and divinity — a material type of the incarnation in which deity clothes humanity, or rather in which divine and human natures are united in one person.
6: Precious stones from Parvaim set into the walls for beauty. The temple's interior beauty is not utilitarian but aesthetically intentional. The new Jerusalem descends 'prepared as a bride adorned for her husband' (Revelation 21:2); its beauty is not ornamentation but the intrinsic glory of God dwelling there.
7: Gold overlay — ceiling, beams, thresholds, walls, doors all overlaid. Gold in the temple functions as light-material, catching and reflecting the lampstand's flame throughout the interior. Christ is 'the radiance of the glory of God' (Hebrews 1:3) — not reflecting another's light but the source of divine radiance itself.
8: The Most Holy Place (qōḏeš haqqŏḏāšîm) — 20 × 20 cubits, overlaid with 600 talents of gold. This innermost chamber, where the ark would rest under the cherubim, was accessible only to the high priest once a year (Lev 16). Hebrews 9:3-8 treats this as the type Christ fulfills: he entered 'not into holy places made with hands, which are copies of the true things, but into heaven itself, now to appear in the presence of God on our behalf' (Hebrews 9:24).
9: The upper chambers overlaid with gold; the nails were of gold, fifty shekels per nail. Even the fasteners are gold — every structural element contributes to the unified whole. The new creation: 'I saw no temple in the city, for its temple is the Lord God the Almighty and the Lamb' (Revelation 21:22). In the eschatological city the entire space is holy of holies.
10: Two sculptured cherubim overlaid with gold stand in the Most Holy Place, guarding the divine throne-space. Christ entered through the guarded threshold of the holy of holies by his own blood (Hebrews 10:19-20); the cherubim that barred Eden's entry (Genesis 3:24) now yield to the one whose blood speaks a better word than Abel's (Hebrews 12:24).
11: Each cherub's wingspan was five cubits; the total wingspan of both cherubim together spanned the entire twenty-cubit Most Holy Place. In Revelation 5:6 the Lamb stands 'in the midst of the throne' surrounded by the four living creatures — the glorified counterparts of the cherubim — as the one worthy to open the scroll of human history.
12: The second cherub's outer wing touched the opposite wall while its inner wing touched the first cherub's wing — the two forming one continuous canopy. The unified cherubim-canopy over the ark represents divine protection over the covenant. 'He will cover you with his feathers; under his wings you will find refuge' (Psalms 91:4) — a poetic form of the same image Christ fulfills: 'how often I desired to gather your children... as a hen gathers her chicks under her wings' (Matthew 23:37).
13: The cherubim stood on their feet, facing the nave — outward, toward approaching worshippers. The guards face the people, not away from them. Christ, as the great high priest, does not turn away from the approach of sinners: 'he is able to save to the uttermost those who draw near to God through him, since he always lives to make intercession for them' (Hebrews 7:25).
14: The veil (pārōḵet) — made of blue, purple, and crimson fabrics with woven cherubim — separated the main hall from the Most Holy Place. The tearing of the temple veil at Christ's death (Matthew 27:51) is the definitive end of this separation: 'we have confidence to enter the holy places by the blood of Jesus, by the new and living way that he opened for us through the curtain, that is, through his flesh' (Hebrews 10:19-20). The veil torn = the flesh broken = access opened.
15: The two pillars Jachin and Boaz — their names (Jachin = 'he establishes'; Boaz = 'in him is strength') form a confessional pair. Christ fulfills both: 'on this rock I will build my church' (Matthew 16:18) — the establishment; 'I can do all things through him who strengthens me' (Philippians 4:13) — the strength.
16: Chains in the inner sanctuary and 100 pomegranates on each chain — the same ornamental fruit-forms adorning the high priest's robe (Exodus 28:33-34), linking priestly vestments to the temple's architectural decoration. Priest and temple are one system — pointing toward Christ who is simultaneously the priest and the temple (John 2:21; Hebrews 4:14).
17: The pillars set before the temple — Jachin on the south, Boaz on the north. Every approach to the temple passed between these confessional pillars: 'established / in-strength.' Revelation 3:12 uses the pillar-image for the overcomer: 'I will make him a pillar in the temple of my God.' Christ is both the pillar-establisher and the one who grants pillar-status to those who conquer in him.
